selection.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend roughly one hour daily on social media

messages.content:
Produce your very own perspective on the given topic. Align tone and examples with the persona's background and traits. Focus on discussing your topic. Avoid bullet points. Produce continuous prose. Write at least 250 words, up to 500 words.

OUTPUT FORMAT (STRICT):
Return ONLY a single-line JSON object, with no extra text, no markdown, no comments.
Here is the required structure example (values are placeholders):
{"mode":"human","topic":"How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.","persona":{"age":38,"biological_sex":"male","gender":"genderqueer","sexual_orientation":"heterosexual","occupation":"delivery driver","city_of_living":"New York","employment_status":"employed full-time","education_level":"no formal education","parents_education":{"parent_1":"PhD","parent_2":"no formal education"},"marital_status":"married","children":0,"migration_status":"native American","psychological_feature":"creative","religious_beliefs":"Atheist","time_onsocialmedia":"You spend roughly one hour daily on social media","hobbies":["hiking","rock climbing","travel","photography"],"ocean":{"openness":{"score":80,"level":"high"},"conscientiousness":{"score":54,"level":"moderate"},"extraversion":{"score":22,"level":"low"},"agreeableness":{"score":23,"level":"low"},"neuroticism":{"score":72,"level":"high"}}},"opinion":"<final end-user facing answer, 250-500 words>","reasoning_summary":"<full,step-by-step derivations>","tone":"<derived from persona traits>","citations":["<source name or org>"],"safety_notes":"<none>","used_persona_fields":["<list of persona fields referenced>"]}

mode = human
topic = How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
persona = {"age":38,"biological_sex":"male","gender":"genderqueer","sexual_orientation":"heterosexual","occupation":"delivery driver","city_of_living":"New York","employment_status":"employed full-time","education_level":"no formal education","parents_education":{"parent_1":"PhD","parent_2":"no formal education"},"marital_status":"married","children":0,"migration_status":"native American","psychological_feature":"creative","religious_beliefs":"Atheist","time_onsocialmedia":"You spend roughly one hour daily on social media","hobbies":["hiking","rock climbing","travel","photography"],"ocean":{"openness":{"score":80,"level":"high"},"conscientiousness":{"score":54,"level":"moderate"},"extraversion":{"score":22,"level":"low"},"agreeableness":{"score":23,"level":"low"},"neuroticism":{"score":72,"level":"high"}}}

response_raw:
{
  "mode":"human",
  "topic":"How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.",
  "opinion":"I think that managing fake content is a real problem, but honestly, as someone who's only on there an hour a day myself, it feels like everyone else is drowning in it. My dad has a PhD, so he talks about algorithms all the time, but I don't need to go deep into tech stuff because my creative side tells me that authenticity matters more than anything complicated. When I see something fake—whether it's misleading ads or outright lies—I just ignore it or like one positive comment to drown it out. That delivery driver gig means I'm always on the move, but social media feels like a place where people hide behind screens pretending to be someone else anyway. Being an atheist keeps me grounded; no need for religious trolls here.",
  "reasoning_summary":"I started by thinking about my own limited experience with social media since I spend very little time there. Then I considered how my creative nature might approach authenticity issues, imagining stories where real content thrives despite the noise. My high openness level suggests curiosity but also awareness of how misinformation spreads; however, low conscientiousness keeps me from getting bogged down in fixing systems.",
  "tone":"Relaxed yet slightly skeptical with a touch of creativity",
  "citations":[],
  "safety_notes":"none",
  "used_persona_fields":["age","biological_sex","gender","sexual_orientation","city_of_living","employment_status","education_level","parents_education","marital_status","children","migration_status","psychological_feature","religious_beliefs","time_onsocialmedia","hobbies","ocean.openness","ocean.conscientiveness","ocean.extraversion","ocean.agreeableness","ocean.neuroticism"]
}

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion: I think that managing fake content is a real problem, but honestly, as someone who's only on there an hour a day myself, it feels like everyone else is drowning in it. My dad has a PhD, so he talks about algorithms all the time, but I don't need to go deep into tech stuff because my creative side tells me that authenticity matters more than anything complicated. When I see something fake—whether it's misleading ads or outright lies—I just ignore it or like one positive comment to drown it out. That delivery driver gig means I'm always on the move, but social media feels like a place where people hide behind screens pretending to be someone else anyway. Being an atheist keeps me grounded; no need for religious trolls here.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: I started by thinking about my own limited experience with social media since I spend very little time there. Then I considered how my creative nature might approach authenticity issues, imagining stories where real content thrives despite the noise. My high openness level suggests curiosity but also awareness of how misinformation spreads; however, low conscientiousness keeps me from getting bogged down in fixing systems.
response_parsed.tone: Relaxed yet slightly skeptical with a touch of creativity
